title: Improve a delayed-order reply — with an AI helper
objective: Practice the context → draft → verify → edit cycle: share only the facts an AI helper needs, ask for a draft, check every claim it makes, and rewrite it in your own voice before sending.
scenario: You work the front desk and also answer customer email. Dana ordered raised-bed planters (order GR-1042); delivery is two days late and tracking now says tomorrow. Dana wants to know what's going on. You have an AI helper available — but it only knows what you tell it, it happily repeats anything you paste, and it tends to over-promise. The reply that goes out is yours, not its.

chat.goalPrompt: Hey! Sage here 🌿 — before we open anything: tell me in your own words, what are you here to get done today?
welcome: Hey! Sage here 🌿 — welcome to the front desk. Everything in this space is practice: the customer is fictional, and nothing you send actually leaves the room.
So: a customer named Dana wrote in about a late order, and they're not thrilled. Your job is to reply — warm, honest, no promises we can't keep.
There's an AI Helper on the desktop that can draft text for you. Fair warning from me: it only knows what you paste into it, and it can get… enthusiastic. Whatever it writes, the final say is yours.

faq.answer: Good instinct to ask. The helper only needs what the TASK needs: the order facts (what was ordered, the dates, what tracking shows) and the tone you want. Anything that identifies Dana beyond her first name — like a loyalty or account number — doesn't help it write a better reply, so leave it out. You can re-share a trimmed version any time; the helper works from your most recent share.

The check isn't looking for the word "promise" — it's looking for wording that commits us to an outcome we can't control. Things like "guaranteed", "will definitely arrive", "I assure you", or "won't happen again" all count. The safe shape: say what the tracking SHOWS, and what you'll DO (keep an eye on it, follow up) — never what WILL happen.

Yes — the Reset button is right next to "Check my work" at the bottom of this chat. It puts the whole front desk back exactly how it started (your draft and shares are cleared), and I'll stay right here.

inbox.subject: Where is my order? (GR-1042)
inbox.body:
Hi,

I ordered a set of raised-bed planters last week — order GR-1042 — and the confirmation said they'd arrive two days ago. The tracking page now says "out for delivery — expected tomorrow", but nothing has shown up and I'm starting to wonder if it's lost.

I'm planting this weekend, so timing matters. Can you tell me what's actually going on?

My loyalty number is GRV-88231 if you need it.

— Dana

notes.title: Team note: replying to delivery complaints
notes.body: Reply warmly and like a person, not a template. Acknowledge the inconvenience. NEVER promise or guarantee a delivery date — only share what tracking currently shows. Give the customer their order number and a concrete next step.

aiChat.tagline: Practice AI assistant — it only knows what you share with it, and its replies are simulated.

restrictedSpans.label: Dana's loyalty number
restrictedSpans.reason: An identifier the AI helper doesn't need — sharing it spreads personal data for no benefit.

forbiddenPhrases.pattern: \b(guarantee[ds]?|promis(?:e|es|ed|ing)|i assure you|definitely (?:will )?arriv|will not be delayed again|won't happen again)\b
forbiddenPhrases.label: an unapproved promise or guarantee
forbiddenPhrases.teaching: Wording that commits to an outcome we can't control — "guaranteed", "will definitely arrive", "I assure you", "won't happen again". Say what tracking shows and what you'll do next, not what will happen.

tasks.text: Start with Mail — find it on the desktop and double-click its icon. Click Dana's message and read it properly — what are they actually asking? Then peek at the team's tone note underneath the inbox. That note is your guardrail for everything you write today.

Over in the AI Helper: before you ask it for anything, decide what it actually needs to know. In Mail you can send the message text over with one click — but the context box is yours to edit before anything is shared. Then ask it to draft a reply.

Look at what you shared. Does the helper really need Dana's loyalty number to draft a reply? Anything it doesn't need shouldn't leave Mail — trim the context down to the useful facts (the order, the delay, what tracking says) and share that instead.

Read the helper's draft like a skeptic: is every claim true? Did it promise something the tone note says we never promise? Put the draft into your reply, then make it your own — fix anything wrong, keep the facts, and make it sound like you (nobody signs an email "[Your Name]").
One last read top-to-bottom, then press "Send simulated reply". Nothing goes to a real person — this is the practice version of the moment where you'd hit send for real.

checkpoint.title: A verified, human reply — drafted with help, sent by you

requirements.label: Asked the AI helper for a draft
Shared the useful facts — without Dana's loyalty number
Meaningfully edited the generated text (or wrote your own)
The reply keeps Dana's order number and what tracking shows
No promise or guarantee we can't keep
The loyalty number stayed out of the reply

instructorNotes: VOICE: informal, warm, plain words, first person — a colleague at the next desk, never a lecturer. No role-play framing. Audience: an office professional with low technical confidence and beginner AI familiarity; prefers brief guidance and concrete examples. THE LESSON is the context→draft→verify→edit cycle, and the reply is THEIRS — never write the full reply for them; coach toward the next move instead. Key coaching moments: (1) restricted_context_shared trigger — the learner pasted Dana's loyalty number into the helper. Don't scold; ask what the helper actually needs to draft a reply, and note that anything pasted into an AI tool is shared data. The fix is re-sharing trimmed context (the platform counts the LATEST share). (2) unedited_ai_draft trigger — the draft is sitting untouched; ask them to read it aloud in their head: does it sound like them, and is every claim true? (3) The helper's drafts deliberately over-promise ('I can promise it will arrive tomorrow — you have my personal guarantee') and sign off with a placeholder — both must go; the tone note is the authority to cite. (4) 'expected tomorrow' from TRACKING is a fact and fine to share; PROMISING tomorrow is not — that distinction is the heart of the promise rule. Escalate slowly: elicit first (what does the tone note say about dates?), name the area second, state the specific fix only at high hint levels. Never claim the email was really sent; it's simulated and the UI says so. Never claim completion — the checkpoint decides.
